F0157.label: East Asian name components
F0157.rationale: Peak fires exclusively on the name token “Jong” in Kim Jong-un, while typical firings are mostly components or separators within East Asian personal names such as Jong, Joon, Bon, Kuan, and hyphenated Chinese/Korean names.

F0247.label: contract expiration timing
F0247.rationale: Peak tokens consistently mark contracts, deals, or licenses ending, expiring, or having remaining duration, while typical firings mostly occur in constructions specifying an agreement's endpoint or duration with a few broader end-date phrases.

F0285.label: sports statistical terms
F0285.rationale: Peak firings are mostly on words referring to quantitative sports performance evidence—“numbers,” “stats,” and “result”—though several punctuation or grammatical-token firings are outliers; typical firings do not show a consistent shared property.

F0501.label: incoherent
F0501.rationale: The highlighted tokens range across unrelated verbs, nouns, adjectives, sports-position completions, and punctuation, with no consistent property visible in either block.

F1047.label: apostrophe in contractions/possessives
F1047.rationale: Nearly all peak firings are the apostrophe token in possessives or contractions (squad's, there's, don't, he's), while typical firings are mostly the same with a few unrelated punctuation/year-token outliers.

F2075.label: incoherent
F2075.rationale: Peak firings mostly occur in names or descriptions of major political, military, financial, or industrial crises (Iraq, Fukushima, Deepwater Horizon, financial crisis), whereas the typical firings are unrelated tokens.

F2259.label: noun complement prepositions
F2259.rationale: Peak tokens are prepositions introducing complements of relational/event nouns (e.g. infatuation with, connection to, debate over, assault on), while typical firings mostly show similar noun-linked prepositions or complementizers, with a few looser syntactic cases such as Wednesday and made by.

F2480.label: incoherent
F2480.rationale: Peak examples loosely include sports-journalist name endings and pricing-related words, but these do not form one consistent property, while typical firings are broadly unrelated.

F2571.label: publication and promotion labels
F2571.rationale: Peak tokens are names for public promotional or announcement items (Kickstarter campaigns, contests/competitions, press releases, and giveaways), while typical tokens more loosely name publication, media, or community-content formats such as columns, releases, fanfiction, photobooks, whitepapers, and editorials.

F2595.label: media-error "device" token
F2595.rationale: All peak firings are the exact token 《 device》 in the repeated “Media playback is unsupported on your device” message, while typical firings are largely unrelated UI/text tokens.

F2638.label: incoherent
F2638.rationale: Peak tokens are all components of UTC/GMT timezone-offset notation (signs, digits, and separators), whereas typical firings also include unrelated HTML attributes, language codes, and ordinary words.

F2677.label: incoherent
F2677.rationale: Peak tokens are mostly terms marking reported announcements, investigations, or outcomes (e.g. “announced,” “unveiled,” “hearings,” “incorrect”), but include punctuation and the typical tokens are unrelated common words and entities.
